heroOverview: Anosmia is the complete loss of the sense of smell, affecting an estimated 3-5% of the general population at any one time and up to 25% of adults over 50. It arises when olfactory receptor neurons in the nasal epithelium, the olfactory nerve fibres passing through the cribriform plate, or the olfactory processing centres of the brain are damaged or blocked. The most common reversible causes are viral upper respiratory infections and chronic rhinosinusitis, while post-viral anosmia surged in recognition during the COVID-19 pandemic when it affected 40-70% of infected individuals. Permanent anosmia carries real safety risks — inability to detect smoke, gas leaks, or spoiled food — and profoundly reduces quality of life by eliminating pleasure from eating and triggering depression in up to 20% of affected people. Early assessment and targeted treatment restore smell in a substantial proportion of cases.

keyStats.prevalence: 3-5% of the general population; up to 25% of adults over 50; post-COVID anosmia affected an estimated 40-70% of symptomatic COVID-19 cases (JAMA, 2020)
keyStats.demographics: Increases with age; men and women equally affected for most causes; higher rates in Parkinson's disease (90%) and Alzheimer's disease (50-90%)
keyStats.avgAge: Can occur at any age; degenerative causes more common over 60; post-viral anosmia common in adults 20-60
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 300-600 million people affected worldwide by COVID-19-associated smell loss at pandemic peak

definition: Anosmia (ICD-10: R43.0) is the complete absence of olfactory function — the inability to detect any odour at all concentration levels. It is distinguished from hyposmia (partial reduction in smell sensitivity) and parosmia (distorted smell). The olfactory system begins at approximately 10 million bipolar receptor neurons lining the roof of the nasal cavity, whose axons pass through tiny openings in the cribriform plate of the ethmoid bone to synapse in the olfactory bulb. Signals then travel via the olfactory tract to the piriform cortex, entorhinal cortex, and amygdala. Anosmia arises from one of three anatomical levels: conductive anosmia when nasal congestion or obstruction physically blocks odorant molecules from reaching the receptor epithelium; sensorineural anosmia when the receptor neurons or their central projections are damaged; and central anosmia when olfactory processing areas of the brain are involved. The condition falls under general medicine with specialist involvement from otolaryngology (ENT), neurology, or neuropsychiatry depending on the underlying cause. Untreated anosmia impairs nutrition (taste is 80% olfactory), removes warning signals, and is significantly associated with depressive symptoms.

typesClassification.description: Physical blockage prevents odorant molecules reaching the olfactory epithelium; causes include chronic rhinosinusitis, nasal polyps, and a deviated nasal septum. This form is most amenable to surgical or medical treatment.

Damage to the olfactory receptor neurons or their central projections, most often from viral infection (including COVID-19), head trauma, or neurodegenerative disease. Recovery is variable and often partial.

Involvement of the olfactory bulb, tract, or cortical processing areas by trauma, tumour, or neurodegeneration. Alzheimer's disease and Parkinson's disease characteristically affect olfactory processing areas early.

Present from birth; may be isolated (total inability to smell, usually discovered in childhood) or part of Kallmann syndrome, which combines anosmia with absent pubertal development due to a developmental defect in olfactory and GnRH neurons.

primarySymptoms: Complete inability to detect any odour — from flowers and coffee to strong irritants such as ammonia — confirmed across multiple sniff tests.
Markedly reduced sense of taste, because approximately 80% of perceived flavour is olfactory; patients commonly report that food has lost all taste and interest.
Inability to detect warning odours — smoke, burning food, gas leaks, and rotten food — creating real safety hazards.
Reduced appetite and unintentional weight loss in some patients because eating becomes unrewarding.
Depressed mood, social withdrawal, and reduced quality of life, which affect approximately 20% of people with persistent anosmia.
In post-viral anosmia, nasal congestion and discharge may accompany the initial infection phase before resolving while the smell loss persists.
In nasal polyp or chronic sinusitis-related anosmia, co-occurring nasal blockage, post-nasal drip, and facial pressure or pain.
In Parkinson's disease-related anosmia, smell loss typically predates motor symptoms by years and may be the first detectable sign of the disease.
Parosmia (distorted smell) or phantosmia (perceiving smells that are absent) may develop during recovery from post-viral anosmia, signalling regenerating olfactory neurons.
In traumatic anosmia, sudden complete smell loss immediately following a head impact, usually without nasal symptoms.
earlyWarningSigns: Gradual decline in the ability to distinguish between similar smells, or needing stronger odours than before to detect them.
Foods tasting flat or bland despite good oral hygiene and normal dental health.
Noticing that familiar environmental smells — a partner's perfume, a pet, or a favourite food — are no longer perceptible.
Persistent nasal congestion that does not respond to decongestants, which may signal chronic rhinosinusitis with polyp formation.
emergencySigns: Sudden complete anosmia following head trauma — seek urgent assessment for cribriform plate fracture and cerebrospinal fluid rhinorrhoea.
Anosmia combined with new severe headache, fever, confusion, or neck stiffness — indicates possible meningitis or encephalitis requiring emergency care.
Anosmia with visual field changes or unilateral visual loss — may indicate a subfrontal meningioma pressing on the olfactory nerve, requiring urgent imaging.
Anosmia in a teenager with failure of puberty — raises concern for Kallmann syndrome and warrants urgent endocrine assessment.

causes.description: Respiratory viruses — including rhinovirus, influenza, and SARS-CoV-2 — directly infect and destroy olfactory receptor neurons or the supporting sustentacular cells that maintain them, causing sudden anosmia. COVID-19 caused anosmia via ACE2 receptor-mediated sustentacular cell infection, often without nasal congestion.

Persistent mucosal oedema and polyp growth physically block access of odorant molecules to the olfactory cleft in the roof of the nasal cavity. This is the most common cause of progressive conductive anosmia and responds well to intranasal corticosteroids and, when necessary, functional endoscopic sinus surgery.

Deceleration forces shear the delicate olfactory filaments passing through the cribriform plate, or damage the olfactory bulb directly. Post-traumatic anosmia occurs in approximately 5% of mild and up to 30% of severe head injuries; it is often permanent because regeneration across the cribriform plate is unreliable.

Parkinson's disease damages the olfactory bulb and anterior olfactory nucleus as the earliest neuropathological change, preceding motor symptoms by an estimated 4-7 years in approximately 90% of cases. Alzheimer's disease similarly reduces olfactory processing in 50-90% of patients, correlating with cognitive decline.

Several drugs damage the olfactory epithelium or alter central processing: intranasal zinc preparations (recalled by the FDA), aminoglycosides, certain chemotherapy agents, and cocaine are established causes. Chronic solvent or heavy metal exposure causes occupational anosmia in industrial settings.

causes.cause: Deviated nasal septum and anatomical obstruction
causes.description: A severely deviated septum or bulky inferior turbinate hypertrophy reduces airflow to the olfactory cleft and limits odorant access; surgical correction can restore smell in approximately 30-50% of such cases.

Rare but important causes include olfactory neuroblastoma (esthesioneuroblastoma), meningiomas pressing on the olfactory tract, and granulomatous conditions such as sarcoidosis or Wegener's granulomatosis involving the nasal mucosa.

riskFactors.description: The number of olfactory receptor neurons declines by roughly 1% per year after age 40; by age 80, over 60% of people have significant smell impairment due to accumulated neuron loss and reduced regenerative capacity.
Men have a modestly higher prevalence of anosmia in population surveys, partly because of higher rates of head trauma and heavier occupational toxic exposures.
Repeated viral URIs cumulatively damage the olfactory epithelium. Vaccination against influenza and COVID-19 reduces the risk of post-viral anosmia from those pathogens.
Uncontrolled chronic nasal inflammation progressively damages the olfactory epithelium over years; optimal medical management and surgical clearance when appropriate reduce this risk.
Olfactory testing is being evaluated as an early screening tool in people at genetic risk of Parkinson's disease; reduced smell sensitivity may precede motor symptoms by years.
High-energy head impacts from contact sports, motor vehicle collisions, or falls are the leading modifiable cause of sudden permanent anosmia.
Cigarette smoke chronically damages olfactory receptor neurons; smokers have significantly higher rates of hyposmia and anosmia than non-smokers, and smell sensitivity partly recovers with cessation.
Prolonged exposure to organic solvents, heavy metals (cadmium, lead), and certain industrial chemicals is an established occupational cause of progressive olfactory loss.

affectedDemographics: Adults over 60, in whom presbyosmia (age-related smell loss) is common
Post-viral patients, including those recovering from COVID-19
People with chronic rhinosinusitis or nasal polyps
Patients with Parkinson's disease (approximately 90%) or Alzheimer's disease (50-90%)
Athletes or individuals with a history of head trauma
Smokers and people with heavy occupational chemical exposure
diagnosisOverview: Assessment of anosmia begins with a detailed history: timing (sudden vs gradual), relation to respiratory infection, trauma, or medication, associated nasal symptoms, and presence of parosmia or phantosmia. A validated psychophysical smell test is essential for objective confirmation and severity grading: the University of Pennsylvania Smell Identification Test (UPSIT), the Sniffin' Sticks test, or the Brief Smell Identification Test are all established tools. A score below the 10th percentile for age and sex confirms anosmia. Nasal endoscopy by an ENT specialist assesses for polyps, mucosal oedema, septal deviation, and sinonasal tumour; this is the primary investigation for suspected conductive anosmia. CT of the paranasal sinuses is the imaging of choice to assess polyp and inflammatory burden, identify structural obstruction, and detect any cribriform plate abnormality. MRI of the brain and olfactory bulbs is reserved for suspected central or traumatic anosmia, cranial nerve tumour, or when the clinical picture suggests neurodegenerative disease. Olfactory bulb volume on MRI is reduced in most post-infectious and neurodegenerative anosmia and has prognostic value. When Parkinson's or Alzheimer's disease is suspected, neurological and neuropsychological assessment is initiated. Blood tests routinely exclude vitamin B12 deficiency and zinc deficiency, both reversible metabolic causes.

diagnosticTests.test: University of Pennsylvania Smell Identification Test (UPSIT) or Sniffin' Sticks
diagnosticTests.purpose: Validated psychophysical tests that quantify smell sensitivity and identify anosmia, hyposmia, or parosmia using standardised scratch-and-sniff or pen-like odorant devices.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You scratch and sniff a series of booklets or sniff labelled pens and choose from multiple-choice answers; the full UPSIT takes about 15 minutes and gives a percentile score for your age and sex.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Direct visualisation of the nasal cavity and olfactory cleft to identify polyps, mucosal oedema, septal deviation, and any mucosal lesion.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A thin flexible camera is passed through each nostril after topical anaesthetic spray; the procedure takes 5-10 minutes and is mildly uncomfortable.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Maps extent of sinusitis, polyps, and structural obstruction, and detects cribriform plate injury or rare sinonasal tumour.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A quick, painless scan of your head taking 2-3 minutes; no contrast is usually needed; you lie still in the scanner.

diagnosticTests.test: MRI brain with olfactory bulb volumetry
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects olfactory bulb atrophy (reduced in post-viral and neurodegenerative anosmia), central lesions, subfrontal meningiomas, and traumatic injury to the olfactory tract.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 30-45 minute MRI scan; coronal sequences through the olfactory bulbs require no contrast and are interpreted against age-matched norms.

diagnosticTests.test: Blood tests (B12, zinc, full blood count, thyroid)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes vitamin B12 and zinc deficiency, hypothyroidism, and blood disorders — all reversible metabolic causes of olfactory impairment.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard arm blood draw; results in 24-48 hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Objective electrophysiological measure of olfactory nerve and cortical responses used in specialist centres to distinguish sensorineural from central anosmia and to monitor recovery.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Electrodes are placed on the scalp while you smell standardised odour pulses; used mainly in research and specialist clinical settings.

treatmentOverview: Treatment is cause-specific and stratified by whether the anosmia is conductive, sensorineural, or central. Conductive anosmia from chronic rhinosinusitis and nasal polyps responds well to a short course of systemic corticosteroids (prednisolone 30 mg/day for 10-14 days) followed by long-term intranasal corticosteroid spray, which is the most evidence-based pharmacological intervention available. When polyps are extensive or medical treatment fails, functional endoscopic sinus surgery (FESS) clears the olfactory cleft and restores airflow; approximately 50-70% of patients recover useful smell after FESS. For sensorineural anosmia — including post-viral, post-traumatic, and idiopathic — olfactory training is the only treatment with replicated positive evidence from randomised trials. Patients sniff four high-intensity odours twice daily for at least 12 weeks; meta-analyses confirm a meaningful improvement in approximately 30-40% of trained patients compared with controls. Olfactory training is safe, inexpensive, and is recommended by European Position Paper on Rhinosinusitis (EPOS) and most ENT societies as first-line treatment for post-infectious anosmia. Emerging treatments under investigation include intranasal platelet-rich plasma injections, vitamin A nasal drops, and intranasal sodium citrate, all of which show modest early signals but lack definitive trial evidence. For neurodegenerative anosmia, there is no specific treatment; managing the underlying disease is the focus.

medicalTreatments.name: Intranasal corticosteroid sprays
medicalTreatments.description: Mometasone, fluticasone, or budesonide nasal sprays reduce mucosal oedema and polyp size in the olfactory cleft; they are first-line maintenance therapy for chronic rhinosinusitis-related anosmia and are used long-term as they have minimal systemic absorption.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Restore partial or complete smell in approximately 40-60% of patients with polyp-related anosmia with continued use; response declines if spray is stopped.

medicalTreatments.name: Short-course systemic corticosteroids
medicalTreatments.description: Prednisolone 25-30 mg orally for 10-14 days reduces acute mucosal inflammation and rapidly deflates nasal polyps; used as a diagnostic test of reversibility and to restore smell temporarily in conductive anosmia.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Restore smell in approximately 60-80% of patients with polyp-related anosmia during the course; effect is typically temporary without ongoing intranasal spray.

medicalTreatments.name: Olfactory training
medicalTreatments.description: Twice-daily sniffing of four standard odours (phenyl ethyl alcohol/rose, eucalyptol, citronellal/lemon, eugenol/cloves) for 12-24 weeks. Training is thought to promote neuroplasticity and receptor neuron regeneration in the olfactory epithelium.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Improves olfactory function in approximately 30-40% of post-viral and idiopathic anosmia patients compared with no training across multiple randomised trials; beneficial in all age groups.

medicalTreatments.name: Intranasal vitamin A drops
medicalTreatments.description: Topical vitamin A (retinol) applied to the olfactory epithelium may support receptor neuron regeneration; early pilot studies showed promise in post-infectious anosmia and there is a plausible neurobiological mechanism.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: One small RCT showed approximately 40% of patients improved vs 20% in placebo; evidence is insufficient for routine recommendation but the treatment is safe and some centres offer it.

medicalTreatments.name: Treatment of underlying allergic rhinitis
medicalTreatments.description: Antihistamines and allergen immunotherapy (for allergic rhinitis-related anosmia) reduce mucosal inflammation that limits odorant access to the olfactory epithelium, restoring smell alongside other allergic symptom control.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Improvement in smell correlates with overall rhinitis control; approximately 30-50% of patients with allergic rhinitis report improved smell after starting effective allergy treatment.

surgicalOptions.name: Functional endoscopic sinus surgery (FESS)
surgicalOptions.description: Endoscopic removal of nasal polyps and diseased sinus mucosa, widening the sinus drainage pathways and the olfactory cleft. Surgery improves airflow to the olfactory epithelium and removes inflammatory tissue that damages receptor neurons.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Restores at least partial smell in approximately 50-70% of patients with chronic rhinosinusitis with polyps when combined with post-operative intranasal corticosteroids.

surgicalOptions.name: Septoplasty and turbinate reduction
surgicalOptions.description: Correction of a severely deviated nasal septum or hypertrophic turbinates to improve airflow to the olfactory cleft; most effective when structural obstruction is the dominant mechanism of conductive anosmia.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Improves smell in approximately 30-50% of patients with significant structural nasal obstruction; outcomes are better when the olfactory epithelium itself is intact.

alternativeTreatments.name: Olfactory training with modified odour sets
alternativeTreatments.description: High-intensity olfactory training — using odours from the patient's personal odour memory rather than fixed sets — has shown slightly higher response rates in some unblinded studies and may improve patient adherence.

alternativeTreatments.name: Acupuncture
alternativeTreatments.description: Small studies suggest acupuncture may modestly improve smell in post-infectious anosmia; evidence quality is low and it is not part of mainstream guidelines, but the treatment is safe.

whySeeSpecialist: A general practitioner can manage mild, self-limited post-viral anosmia, but an ENT specialist should be involved when anosmia has persisted more than 8-12 weeks, when there is any nasal obstruction or polyp suspected on examination, or when imaging is needed. A neurologist should assess anosmia combined with tremor, memory change, or other neurological signs, because smell loss can be the earliest detectable marker of Parkinson's or Alzheimer's disease. Any sudden anosmia following head trauma needs urgent imaging.
doctorSelectionGuide: An ENT surgeon with a specific interest in rhinology and smell disorders — ideally with access to a dedicated smell clinic using validated psychophysical tests and olfactory bulb MRI — offers the most thorough assessment. For post-viral or idiopathic anosmia, a rhinologist familiar with olfactory training protocols is valuable. When neurodegenerative disease is suspected, a neurologist or geriatrician with access to neuropsychological testing should be involved. Ask about olfactory training guidance, duration of follow-up, and access to FESS if surgical treatment is needed.
preventionStrategies: Vaccination against influenza and COVID-19 reduces risk of post-viral anosmia from those pathogens.
Wear appropriate protective headgear during contact sports and when cycling or motorcycling.
Manage chronic rhinosinusitis and allergic rhinitis proactively with intranasal corticosteroids and allergen avoidance to limit ongoing epithelial damage.
Stop smoking — olfactory sensitivity partly recovers after cessation and tobacco cessation reduces cumulative nerve damage.
Use appropriate respiratory protection in workplaces with organic solvent or heavy metal exposure.
lifestyleModifications: Conduct daily olfactory training with a set of four strong odours; set a phone reminder to maintain twice-daily adherence.
Install smoke and carbon monoxide detectors and check them monthly — anosmia removes a critical safety warning system.
Use visual and tactile cues to check for food spoilage — rely on use-by dates, colour changes, and texture rather than smell.
Experiment with flavour-enhancing techniques: stronger herbs and spices, varied textures, and temperature contrasts can improve food enjoyment despite smell loss.
Discuss mood changes with a doctor — depression secondary to anosmia responds well to treatment and social support.

recommended: Zinc-rich foods (oysters, pumpkin seeds, meat) to support olfactory receptor neuron function — zinc deficiency is a reversible cause of smell loss
Vitamin A-rich foods (liver, orange and yellow vegetables) to support mucosal and receptor neuron maintenance
Strong-flavoured and highly textured foods to maximise residual sensory input during anosmia
A varied, nutrient-dense diet to prevent unintentional weight loss from reduced appetite
avoid: Excessively bland foods that provide little sensory stimulation and reduce motivation to eat
Overuse of salt or sugar to compensate for lost flavour, which creates cardiovascular and metabolic risk

exerciseGuidelines: Moderate aerobic exercise is recommended and may modestly support neuroplasticity relevant to olfactory recovery. There is no specific exercise restriction for anosmia. Patients should avoid contact sports or activities with high head-injury risk until smell has recovered or until the risk is formally accepted. Olfactory training exercises should continue regardless of physical activity levels.
dailyManagement: Perform olfactory training twice daily — morning and evening — sniffing each of four odours for 10 seconds with a 10-second break between each.
Check smoke and CO detectors weekly.
Keep a smell diary noting any fleeting perceptions, which signal possible recovery and motivate continued training.
Continue intranasal corticosteroid spray consistently if prescribed — missing doses allows polyps and inflammation to return.
Report new parosmia (distorted smells) to your clinician — it is usually a positive sign of olfactory nerve regeneration.
prognosis: Prognosis depends heavily on the underlying cause. Post-viral anosmia from non-COVID viral infections recovers spontaneously in approximately 60-70% of patients within one year, with or without olfactory training; COVID-19-related anosmia has a similar spontaneous recovery rate, with most improvement occurring in the first 6 months. Conductive anosmia from polyps or sinusitis recovers in 50-70% after surgery and corticosteroids. Post-traumatic anosmia recovers in only about 10-30% of cases, as cribriform plate axon regeneration is unreliable; prognosis is worse for complete vs partial loss at onset. Neurodegenerative anosmia does not improve and worsens with disease progression. Persistent anosmia beyond 2 years is unlikely to recover further. Olfactory bulb volume on MRI is the strongest imaging predictor of recovery — larger residual bulb volume predicts better outcome.
recoveryTimeline: Most post-viral anosmia cases show the greatest improvement in the first 3-6 months. Olfactory training, if started within the first 3 months, improves the probability and speed of recovery. By 12 months, approximately 60-70% of post-viral anosmia patients have recovered useful smell. Recovery from polyp-related conductive anosmia begins within 2-4 weeks of starting intranasal corticosteroids. Post-traumatic anosmia has its maximum recovery window in the first 12 months, after which further improvement is unlikely.
complications: Safety hazards — inability to detect smoke, gas leaks, and spoiled food is the most serious practical complication and the reason smoke detectors and CO alarms are essential.
Depression and anxiety — approximately 20% of people with persistent anosmia develop clinically significant mood disorders related to reduced quality of life and food enjoyment.
Unintentional weight loss and nutritional deficiency — loss of appetite from reduced food pleasure can lead to inadequate caloric and nutrient intake over months.
Social withdrawal — the inability to smell a partner's body, a baby, or familiar environments can impair intimacy and sense of identity.
Parosmia during recovery — distorted smells during olfactory regeneration can be severe enough to make eating intolerable and cause significant distress.

supportResources.name: AbScent — Anosmia and Smell Disorders Charity
supportResources.description: UK charity providing olfactory training guidance, peer support, and research updates for people with anosmia and parosmia.

supportResources.name: Fifth Sense — Smell and Taste Disorders Charity
supportResources.description: Charity supporting people with smell and taste disorders, with practical guides, a helpline, and research news.

supportResources.name: NIH — Smell Disorders
supportResources.description: National Institute on Deafness and Other Communication Disorders patient guide on smell loss, causes, and treatment.

sources.title: Hummel T et al. Effects of olfactory training in patients with olfactory loss. The Laryngoscope, 2009
Addison AB et al. Clinical Olfactory Working Group consensus statement on the treatment of post-infectious olfactory dysfunction. Journal of Allergy and Clinical Immunology, 2021
Fokkens WJ et al. EPOS 2020: European Position Paper on Rhinosinusitis and Nasal Polyps. Rhinology, 2020
Whitcroft KL, Hummel T. Olfactory Dysfunction in COVID-19: Diagnosis and Management. JAMA, 2020
Doty RL. Olfactory dysfunction in neurodegenerative diseases. Nature Reviews Neurology, 2012
NHS — Lost or changed sense of smell

confusedWithConditions.name: Parosmia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Parosmia is a distortion of smell — odours are perceived but wrongly identified or smell unpleasant — whereas anosmia is the complete absence of smell. Parosmia often coexists with or follows anosmia during recovery.

confusedWithConditions.name: Parageusia (taste disturbance)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Parageusia is a distorted sense of taste from changes in taste receptor function; anosmia reduces flavour perception because 80% of flavour is olfactory, but true taste (sweet, salty, sour, bitter, umami) is usually preserved in anosmia.

confusedWithConditions.name: Upper respiratory infection
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: A viral URI causes temporary nasal congestion that transiently reduces smell; post-viral anosmia persists after nasal congestion has resolved, indicating sensorineural rather than conductive dysfunction.

relatedConditions.name: Parosmia
relatedConditions.relevance: Parosmia frequently develops during recovery from anosmia, particularly post-viral, and represents early olfactory nerve regeneration.

relatedConditions.name: Chronic sinusitis
relatedConditions.relevance: The most common underlying cause of persistent conductive anosmia; treatment of sinusitis typically restores smell.

relatedConditions.name: Nasal polyps
relatedConditions.relevance: Polyps block odorant access to the olfactory cleft and are a key surgical target when medical treatment of anosmia fails.

relatedConditions.name: Parkinson's disease
relatedConditions.relevance: Olfactory loss is the earliest detectable sign of Parkinson's disease in approximately 90% of patients, preceding motor symptoms by years.

faqs.question: What causes a sudden loss of smell?
faqs.answer: The most common causes of sudden complete smell loss are viral upper respiratory infections (including COVID-19), which damage olfactory receptor neurons, and head trauma, which shears the olfactory nerve fibres passing through the skull base.

faqs.question: Can you get your sense of smell back after COVID-19?
faqs.answer: Yes. About 60-70% of people who lose their sense of smell after COVID-19 recover it within 6-12 months. Olfactory training — sniffing four strong odours twice daily — speeds and improves recovery and is recommended by ENT guidelines.

faqs.question: How long does post-viral anosmia last?
faqs.answer: Most post-viral smell loss improves within 3-6 months. About 60-70% of patients recover useful smell within one year. A minority have persistent anosmia beyond one year, which is less likely to resolve further.

faqs.question: What is olfactory training and does it work?
faqs.answer: Olfactory training involves sniffing four high-concentration odours — typically rose, lemon, clove, and eucalyptus — for 10 seconds each, twice daily, for at least 12 weeks. Multiple trials confirm it improves smell in approximately 30-40% of patients with post-viral or idiopathic anosmia.

faqs.question: Can nasal polyps cause loss of smell?
faqs.answer: Yes. Nasal polyps are a common cause of progressive smell loss because they grow in the olfactory cleft and block odour molecules from reaching the smell receptors. Intranasal corticosteroids often shrink polyps and restore smell; surgery is used when sprays fail.

faqs.question: Is anosmia a sign of Parkinson's disease?
faqs.answer: It can be. Reduced or absent smell is one of the earliest signs of Parkinson's disease, present in approximately 90% of patients, often appearing 4-7 years before motor symptoms. Anosmia alone is not diagnostic, but when combined with constipation, sleep disturbance, or a family history, it warrants neurological assessment.

faqs.question: Can you smell anything if you have anosmia?
faqs.answer: By definition, complete anosmia means no odours can be detected at any concentration. Some people with partial anosmia (hyposmia) can detect very strong or irritant odours such as ammonia but not subtler smells. A validated smell test distinguishes complete from partial loss.

faqs.question: Does anosmia affect taste?
faqs.answer: Yes, significantly. About 80% of what we call taste is actually olfactory — odours travel to smell receptors from the back of the throat. People with anosmia typically find that food tastes bland or flat even though the tongue's detection of sweet, salty, sour, and bitter is usually intact.

faqs.question: How is anosmia diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis uses a validated smell identification test such as the UPSIT or Sniffin' Sticks, which takes about 15 minutes and gives a percentile score. Nasal endoscopy checks for polyps or obstruction. CT of the sinuses and MRI of the olfactory bulbs are used when structural or neurological causes are suspected.

faqs.question: Is there a medication for anosmia?
faqs.answer: For conductive anosmia from polyps or sinusitis, intranasal and short oral corticosteroids are effective treatments. For sensorineural anosmia after viral infection, olfactory training is the only treatment with good evidence. There is no approved drug that regenerates olfactory receptor neurons.

faqs.question: Can surgery restore your sense of smell?
faqs.answer: Functional endoscopic sinus surgery (FESS) removes nasal polyps and clears sinus obstruction, restoring smell in approximately 50-70% of patients with chronic rhinosinusitis when combined with post-operative intranasal corticosteroids. Surgery does not help sensorineural or central anosmia.

faqs.question: Is losing your sense of smell dangerous?
faqs.answer: Yes, in practical terms. Anosmia removes the ability to detect smoke, gas leaks, and spoiled food — all important safety warnings. Install working smoke and carbon monoxide detectors, check food use-by dates carefully, and inform your household. Depression from quality-of-life loss is also a real risk.

faqs.question: Can stress or anxiety cause anosmia?
faqs.answer: Psychological stress alone does not cause true anosmia, but it can worsen perception of smell in people with anxiety disorders. True anosmia has a structural or physiological cause. However, living with anosmia frequently causes anxiety and depression, which should be assessed and treated.

faqs.question: What is the difference between anosmia and parosmia?
faqs.answer: Anosmia is the complete absence of smell. Parosmia is a distortion — smells are detected but wrongly identified or perceived as unpleasant. Parosmia often develops during recovery from anosmia and is considered a sign that olfactory nerve fibres are regenerating, though the regeneration is initially imperfect.

faqs.question: Can anosmia be congenital (present from birth)?
faqs.answer: Yes. Isolated congenital anosmia affects roughly 1 in 10,000 people. Kallmann syndrome combines congenital anosmia with failure of puberty due to a developmental defect affecting both the olfactory and reproductive systems; it requires specialist endocrine and ENT assessment.

faqs.question: Does smoking cause loss of smell?
faqs.answer: Yes. Chronic cigarette smoke damages olfactory receptor neurons and reduces smell sensitivity. Smokers have significantly higher rates of hyposmia and anosmia than non-smokers. Smell sensitivity partly recovers after stopping smoking, though not always fully.

faqs.question: Can a vitamin deficiency cause anosmia?
faqs.answer: Zinc deficiency is a well-established reversible cause of smell loss; zinc supplementation restores function when deficiency is confirmed. Vitamin B12 deficiency and hypothyroidism can also impair smell. A blood test identifies these treatable metabolic causes.

faqs.question: How do I cope with parosmia during smell recovery?
faqs.answer: Parosmia — when foods smell distorted or disgusting — can be managed by temporarily avoiding trigger foods (often coffee, onions, meat, and eggs), eating cold or room-temperature foods which release fewer volatile compounds, and continuing olfactory training. Most parosmia resolves as nerve regeneration completes, though it can take 12-24 months.

faqs.question: When should I see a doctor about loss of smell?
faqs.answer: See a doctor if smell loss follows a head injury, if it persists beyond 4 weeks after a viral illness, if it is accompanied by nasal blockage, facial pain, or bloody discharge, or if it occurs alongside tremor, memory problems, or other neurological symptoms.

faqs.question: Can allergies cause anosmia?
faqs.answer: Allergic rhinitis can cause significant smell reduction through nasal congestion and mucosal swelling that blocks odorant access to the olfactory cleft. Complete anosmia from allergy alone is uncommon; more often allergic rhinitis causes hyposmia that improves with antihistamines and intranasal corticosteroids.

metaDescription: Anosmia is the complete loss of smell, commonly caused by viral infection, nasal polyps, or head trauma. Learn diagnosis, olfactory training, and recovery.
